notes: MB embedded_mbid confirms Chet Baker, 1989, A&M Records, full tracklist. albumDNA is verbatim from the matched Wikipedia page ('1989 studio album by Chet Baker'). All 5 keyTracks present in MB tracklist (MB also lists a 6th, 'El Morro'). Claim 'recorded in 1977 / released posthumously 1989' supported by wiki extract.

MB search match (sim 1.0) confirms Geri Allen, 1989, Minor Music (DE). First DNA sentence verbatim from matched wiki page. 'jazz legends on equal terms / intergenerational dialogue' is editorial framing, not a checkable factual claim, so not flagged. All 6 keyTracks present in MB tracklist.

MB embedded_mbid confirms Kenny Dorham, 1989, Black Lion. All 5 keyTracks (incl. 'Avars (take 3)', 'Stella by Starlight (take 1)') present in MB tracklist; the two tracks named in the DNA both exist. Remaining DNA is editorial. No wikipedia evidence (opensearch returned nothing) and no URL stored.

No MB release survived (search returned null). Stored wiki URL is the Bix Beiderbecke PERSON/biography page, not a page about this album/compilation; the biographical claims in the DNA (most influential jazz soloist of the 1920s, cornet, lyrical approach, 'shooting bullets at a bell') are lifted verbatim from that artist page and are consistent with the right artist. But the album itself as a released entity cannot be verified: no release evidence, and the album-specific framing 'a contemporary jazz album by Bix Beiderbecke from 1990' is anachronistic for a 1920s cornetist (this is presumably a 1990 reissue/compilation of 78-era recordings) and unconfirmed; label is 'Unknown'. wikiLinkVerdict 'unsure' because the link is to the correct artist but to a bio page, not the album page. keyTracks is null in ours. The DNA prose is also truncated mid-quote ('shooting bullets at a bel').

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Double Vision is a 1986 album by Bob James and David Sanborn. The album was a successful smooth jazz release receiving frequent airplay. The original album was released in the US on May 19, 1986, by Warner Bros Records.
wrongClaims.problem: albumDNA prose describes a DIFFERENT album ('Double Vision', the 1986 Bob James / David Sanborn collaboration), not the self-titled 'David Sanborn' album under verification. The MB release and the matched wiki page (the self-titled Sanborn album, tracks Indio/Smile/Mamacita/Herbs/Concrete Boogie) are the real entity; the prose's factual skeleton (title, 1986, Bob James, May 19 1986 release) belongs to Double Vision.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Sanborn is an album by jazz saxophonist David Sanborn, released by Warner Bros. in 1976. It contains a version of "I Do It for Your Love" by Paul Simon.

notes: Frankenstein record: MB embedded_mbid and keyTracks correctly identify the self-titled David Sanborn album (Warner Bros.; MB dates this release 1990, a reissue; matched wiki page calls the original a 1976 album), but the albumDNA prose is entirely about 'Double Vision' (Bob James & David Sanborn, 1986). All 5 keyTracks match the MB tracklist of the self-titled album. wikiLinkVerdict 'correct' because the stored page IS about the self-titled David Sanborn album (our entity), even though it gives 1976 vs ours/MB 1990 (reissue). Year field (1990) not flagged as error since MB also shows 1990 for this release.

MB embedded_mbid confirms Billy Harper (credited 'Billy Harper Quintet'), 1990, SteepleChase. First DNA sentence verbatim from matched wiki page ('recorded in 1989 ... released on SteepleChase'). All 5 keyTracks present in MB tracklist. Remaining DNA is editorial.

MB embedded_mbid confirms Charlie Parker, 1990, Verve, and an exact 4-track match (Jam Blues / What Is This Thing Called Love / Ballad Medley / Funky Blues) — this is the Norman Granz Verve jam session under Parker. The two tracks named in the DNA both appear in MB. The stored wiki URL, however, resolves to a DIFFERENT album: 'Jam Session (album)' = 1954 live EmArcy album by Clifford Brown, Clark Terry and Maynard Ferguson. That page is discarded for fact-checking (wrong entity) and recorded here as a wrong wiki link the site is shipping. Our prose itself is about the right entity per MB, so verdict is clean with wikiLinkVerdict=wrong_entity.

MB embedded_mbid confirms Dave Weckl, 1990-08-21, GRP. DNA verbatim from matched wiki page, including the 'No. 9 on Billboard's Top Contemporary Jazz Albums chart' claim (present in wiki extract). All 5 keyTracks present in MB tracklist.

MB embedded_mbid confirms Michel Camilo, 1990 (MB lists no label; ours.label field = 'Unknown'). DNA first sentence verbatim from matched wiki page, including 'released in 1990 by Epic Records' (Epic label supported by wiki, the entity-matched source). All 5 keyTracks present in MB tracklist.

MB embedded_mbid confirms Pat Metheny with Dave Holland and Roy Haynes, 1990, Geffen. DNA verbatim from matched wiki page, including the '1991 Grammy Award for Best Instrumental Composition for Change of Heart' claim (present in wiki extract). All 5 keyTracks present in MB tracklist.

MB embedded_mbid confirms the exact entity: artists Dizzy Gillespie, Roy Eldridge, Harry 'Sweets' Edison, Clark Terry, Joe Turner; 1990; Original Jazz Classics; all 4 keyTracks match. DNA correctly names all those personnel. Clark Terry (ours.artist) is among the credited artists. 'recorded in 1974 ... released on the Pablo label' is not directly in MB evidence (MB shows the 1990 OJC reissue, catalog 'OJCCD-497-2 (2310-717)' which embeds the original Pablo number 2310-717), so it is consistent with a standard OJC reissue of a 1974 Pablo date and not contradicted. No wiki evidence/URL. Second DNA sentence is editorial/templated.

Same release as 'the-trumpet-kings-meet-joe-turner' (identical MB mbid 889004a1), credited here to Dizzy Gillespie, who is the lead credited artist in MB. MB confirms artists, 1990, Original Jazz Classics, and all 4 keyTracks. 'recorded in 1974 / Pablo label' consistent with the OJC reissue (catalog embeds Pablo 2310-717), not contradicted by evidence. No wiki evidence/URL. Second DNA sentence is templated editorial.

Same release as the two prior items (identical MB mbid 889004a1), credited here to Roy Eldridge, who is among the credited artists in MB. MB confirms artists, 1990, Original Jazz Classics, all 4 keyTracks. 'recorded in 1974 / Pablo label' consistent with the OJC reissue (catalog embeds Pablo 2310-717), not contradicted. No wiki evidence/URL. Second DNA sentence is templated editorial.

MB search match (sim 1.0) confirms Sonny Sharrock, 1991, Axiom, and all 4 keyTracks match the MB tracklist. But the DNA makes substantive factual claims that the pack evidence cannot check: personnel 'produced by Bill Laswell, features Pharoah Sanders, Elvin Jones, and Charnett Moffett' (MB search match carries no personnel/credits; no wiki page) and 'Sharrock died just three years later' (no biographical evidence in pack). These are unverifiable against available evidence (not contradicted, just uncheckable). My own knowledge is not a citable source. Remaining text ('magnum opus', 'savage distortion-drenched guitar', 'greatest jazz guitar album') is editorial. Hence unverifiable rather than clean.

MB embedded_mbid confirms the entity: title 'Bix Beiderbecke and Frankie Trumbauer - Volume One', 1991, JSP Records, with Eddie Lang among the credited artists (alongside Bix Beiderbecke and His Gang, Frankie Trumbauer and His Orchestra, Broadway Bell-Hops). All 5 keyTracks (Trumbology, Clarinet Marmalade, Singin' the Blues, Ostrich Walk, Riverboat Shuffle) match the MB tracklist; the three tracks named in the DNA all exist. DNA call 'an early jazz recording' is accurate for this 1991 compilation of 1920s recordings. ours.artist 'Eddie Lang' is editorially a minor credit (the set is principally Bix/Trumbauer) but Lang IS a credited artist, so not a factual error. MB releaseGroup secondaryType 'Soundtrack' is anomalous metadata, irrelevant to the claims. No wiki evidence/URL.

MB embedded_mbid confirms James Blood Ulmer, 1991 (recorded 1990), DIW. DNA verbatim from matched wiki page ('recorded in 1990 ... released on the Japanese DIW label'). All 5 keyTracks present in MB tracklist. Remaining DNA is templated editorial.

MB search match (sim 1.0) confirms Coleman Hawkins, 1991, Commodore Records, with all 5 keyTracks matching the MB tracklist; the two tracks named in the DNA ('Smack', 'Smack (alt take)') both exist. This is a 1991 reissue of 1940s Commodore sessions; the templated 'early jazz / contemporary era' phrasing is loose editorial but contains no contradicted factual claim. No wiki evidence/URL.

MB search match (sim 1.0) confirms a Nicky Skopelitis / Sonny Sharrock collaboration, 1991, CMP Records; all 5 keyTracks match the MB tracklist. DNA first sentence verbatim from matched wiki page ('1991 studio album by Nicky Skopelitis and Sonny Sharrock'). 'free jazz context' is editorial. ours.artist credits Sharrock only; the album is a duo, but naming a co-credited leader is legitimate, not a mismatch.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Night Song is an album by the saxophonist Arthur Blythe, released via Clarity Recordings in 1997.
wrongClaims.problem: albumDNA opens by describing a DIFFERENT album ('Night Song', 1997, Clarity Recordings), not 'Hipmotism' (1991, enja) which is the item under verification. The title, label and year in the prose belong to the Night Song album. MB embedded_mbid confirms the real entity is Hipmotism on enja with firstReleaseDate 1991.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Night Song is an album by the saxophonist Arthur Blythe, released via Clarity Recordings in 1997.

notes: Same artist (Arthur Blythe), wrong album grafted into the prose. MB embedded_mbid confirms Hipmotism, enja, releaseGroup firstReleaseDate 1991, and all 5 keyTracks match the MB Hipmotism tracklist (Dear Dessa, Dance Benita Dance, Cousin Sidney, Shadows, Hipmotism). The stored wiki URL ALSO points to the wrong entity: 'Night Song (Arthur Blythe album)' (1997). The second DNA sentence correctly references 'this Enja Records date features Dear Dessa' (Dear Dessa is on Hipmotism per MB), but the opening factual skeleton is Night Song's. wikiLinkVerdict=wrong_entity. keyTracks themselves are correct, so keyTracksVerdict=ok.

Despite 'In Private' being cited in the judge brief as a known disease example (Dusty Springfield content substituted for Art Tatum), THIS item's MB embedded_mbid is authoritative and confirms the real entity: Art Tatum, 1991, Fresh Sound Records (FSRCD 368), with all 5 keyTracks (Wrap Your Troubles in Dreams, Sittin' an' a Rockin', You're Drivin' Me Crazy, Tenderly, Over the Rainbow) matching the MB tracklist exactly. The DNA correctly describes an Art Tatum solo-piano Fresh Sound 1991 reissue and names two of those tracks. The only unverifiable claim is 'originally released on Pablo Records in 1976' (MB shows only the 1991 Fresh Sound release; not in evidence, but not contradicted). Entity is strongly confirmed, no contradictions, so clean; flagging the Pablo-1976 origin as unverified background. No wiki page (opensearch null).
